title: ReadyMCAT Introductory Diagnostic
description: A short, first-launch diagnostic that samples across all 31 AAMC content categories. Its output is a per-topic proficiency PRIOR used to personalize study ordering and prerequisite placement. It is NOT a readiness score and is never shown to the student as a number. See README.md for the prior-mapping spec and the coverage-vs-fatigue rationale.
purpose: Seed each AAMC content category's weakness estimate (so the points-at-stake order is useful from session one) and place the student in the prerequisite graph.

taxonomy.note: Category IDs and names in this file are copied verbatim from taxonomy.json so they join cleanly to topic_weight and the deck-tag mappings.

administration.item_format: single-best-answer multiple choice (4 options); discrete (non-passage) items chosen for unambiguous auto-grading

short.selection_rule: administer the first item listed for each category (item suffix -01)

extended.selection_rule: administer every item in the bank

administration.coverage_vs_fatigue: First-launch fatigue is the binding constraint, so the default is one item per category (full breadth, minimum length). A second item is authored only for the six highest-weight content categories (AAMC weight >= 3.2: 1A,1B,1C,1D,3A,3B) because the points-at-stake order (weight x weakness) is most sensitive to estimation noise where weight is high; the extra item halves the variance of the prior exactly where it most changes ordering. Even two items per category is far too few for a reliable per-category ability estimate, which is why results are consumed as a heavily-shrunk PRIOR, never as a score (see README.md).

honesty_rule: A short quiz is a prior, not a readiness verdict. These results inform card ordering (points-at-stake) and prerequisite-graph placement ONLY. They never feed the dashboard's memory/performance/readiness scores, which obey their own give-up rule (>=200 graded reviews and >=50% coverage).

authoring_integrity.policy: All items are ORIGINAL, authored for ReadyMCAT and grounded in free/openly-licensed sources cited per item.
authoring_integrity.no_leakage: No item is copied or paraphrased from any copyrighted or paid question bank (UWorld, Kaplan, Blueprint, AAMC paid/official prep). No confidential AAMC exam content is reproduced; only the public 'What's on the MCAT Exam?' content outline is used, and only for the topic list (the category IDs/names already encoded in taxonomy.json).
authoring_integrity.deck_independence: Diagnostic items are authored independently of the study deck (Aidan/community decks). The diagnostic and the deck share no items, so studying one cannot leak answers to the other.

content_license: Original diagnostic items (c) ReadyMCAT, released under CC BY-SA 4.0. Each item cites the open source whose concept it is grounded in; facts/concepts are not copyrightable, and no source's expression is reproduced.

aamc_content_outline.name: AAMC 'What's on the MCAT Exam?' content outline
aamc_content_outline.license: Free public outline; AAMC-copyrighted. NOT openly licensed.
aamc_content_outline.attribution: AAMC content outline, used for the public topic list only.
aamc_content_outline.usage: Canonical source for the 31 content-category IDs/names (already encoded in taxonomy.json). No AAMC questions, passages, or confidential content used.

coverage.rule: 2 items for content categories with AAMC weight >= 3.2; 1 item otherwise.

items.category_name: Structure and function of proteins and their constituent amino acids
items.stem: At physiological pH (~7.4), the alpha-amino group of a typical free amino acid is best described as:

options.text: Covalently bonded to the side chain

source.location: Amino acids and proteins (acid-base behavior of functional groups)

items.rationale: The alpha-amino group has a pKa near 9-10, so at pH 7.4 it is mostly protonated (-NH3+), while the alpha-carboxyl (pKa ~2) is deprotonated.

items.category_name: Structure and function of proteins and their constituent amino acids
items.stem: A mutation replaces a valine buried in the hydrophobic core of a globular protein with glutamate. The most likely structural consequence is:

options.text: No effect, because both residues are nonpolar
Increased stability from new hydrophobic contacts
Destabilization or misfolding, because a charged residue is poorly accommodated in the core
Automatic formation of a stabilizing disulfide bond

source.location: Proteins: protein structure and the effect of amino acid R-groups

items.rationale: Glutamate carries a charged, polar side chain; burying it in a hydrophobic interior is energetically unfavorable and tends to disrupt folding/stability.

items.category_name: Transmission of genetic information from the gene to the protein
items.stem: During translation, the mRNA codon is directly recognized by base pairing with the:

source.location: Gene expression: translation (codon-anticodon pairing)

items.rationale: Each tRNA anticodon base-pairs with the complementary mRNA codon to deliver the correct amino acid.

items.category_name: Transmission of genetic information from the gene to the protein
items.stem: A single nucleotide is deleted near the start of a protein-coding sequence. The most likely effect on the encoded protein is:

options.text: A silent change with no effect on the protein
A frameshift that alters most downstream codons
A single conservative amino acid substitution only

source.location: Mutations: frameshift vs. point mutations

items.rationale: A single-base insertion or deletion shifts the reading frame, changing every codon downstream of the change.

items.category_name: Transmission of heritable information from generation to generation and the processes that increase genetic diversity
items.stem: Two genes on the same chromosome are 50 map units (centimorgans) apart. In a testcross, their inheritance pattern will be:

options.text: Completely linked, producing only parental types

source.location: Linkage and recombination (map distance and recombination frequency)

items.rationale: A recombination frequency of 50% is the maximum observed and is indistinguishable from genes assorting independently.

items.category_name: Transmission of heritable information from generation to generation and the processes that increase genetic diversity
items.stem: Crossing over between homologous chromosomes, a major source of genetic diversity, occurs during:

source.location: Meiosis: synapsis and crossing over in prophase I

items.rationale: Homologous chromosomes synapse and exchange segments during prophase I, increasing recombinant diversity.

items.category_name: Principles of bioenergetics and fuel molecule metabolism
items.stem: In eukaryotic cells, the electron transport chain that produces most ATP is located in the:

source.location: Cellular respiration: oxidative phosphorylation

items.rationale: Oxidative phosphorylation and the ETC occur at the inner mitochondrial membrane; glycolysis occurs in the cytosol.

items.category_name: Principles of bioenergetics and fuel molecule metabolism
items.stem: A metabolic reaction has a standard free-energy change of dG = -30 kJ/mol. This reaction is:

options.text: At equilibrium with no net direction
Impossible unless coupled to ATP hydrolysis

source.location: Metabolism and bioenergetics: free energy and spontaneity

items.rationale: A negative dG means the reaction is exergonic and spontaneous; enzymes change the rate, not the sign of dG.

items.category_name: Assemblies of molecules, cells, and groups of cells within single cellular and multicellular organisms
items.stem: The plasma membrane is described as a 'fluid mosaic' primarily because:

options.text: Lipids and many proteins diffuse laterally within a phospholipid bilayer
It is a rigid crystalline lattice of proteins
It is composed almost entirely of cholesterol
Ions cross the hydrophobic core freely without channels

source.location: Structure and function of plasma membranes (fluid mosaic model)

items.rationale: The model captures lateral mobility of lipids and proteins ('fluid') within a heterogeneous bilayer ('mosaic').

items.category_name: The structure, growth, physiology, and genetics of prokaryotes and viruses
items.stem: Which feature is characteristic of typical bacterial (prokaryotic) cells but absent from animal (eukaryotic) cells?

source.location: Prokaryotic cell structure (peptidoglycan cell wall)

items.rationale: Peptidoglycan walls are a bacterial hallmark; animal cells lack cell walls, and prokaryotes (not eukaryotes) lack a membrane-bound nucleus.

items.category_name: Processes of cell division, differentiation, and specialization
items.stem: Cell-cycle checkpoints that halt a cell with damaged DNA before it divides act primarily at the:

source.location: Cell cycle: regulation and checkpoints

items.rationale: The G1/S and G2/M checkpoints gate progression on DNA integrity and replication completeness.

items.category_name: Structure and functions of the nervous and endocrine systems and ways these systems coordinate the organ systems
items.stem: The resting membrane potential of a typical neuron is maintained mainly by:

options.text: The Na+/K+ ATPase together with K+ leak channels
Voltage-gated calcium channels at the axon terminal

source.location: Nervous tissue: the membrane potential

items.rationale: The Na+/K+ pump maintains ion gradients and K+ leak channels make the membrane most permeable to K+, setting ~-70 mV.

items.category_name: Structure and functions of the nervous and endocrine systems and ways these systems coordinate the organ systems
items.stem: Compared with neuronal signaling, endocrine (hormonal) signaling is generally:

options.text: Slower in onset but longer in duration
Faster in onset and shorter in duration

source.location: Endocrine system vs. nervous system signaling

items.rationale: Hormones travel through the bloodstream to target receptors, giving slower but more sustained effects than rapid synaptic transmission.

items.category_name: Structure and integrative functions of the main organ systems
items.stem: Oxygenated blood returning from the lungs enters which heart chamber first?

source.location: Cardiovascular system: the heart and pulmonary circuit

items.rationale: Pulmonary veins carry oxygenated blood from the lungs to the left atrium, then to the left ventricle for systemic delivery.

items.category_name: Structure and integrative functions of the main organ systems
items.stem: In a healthy nephron, essentially all filtered glucose is normally reabsorbed in the:

options.text: Descending limb of the loop of Henle

source.location: Urinary system: tubular reabsorption

items.rationale: Secondary active transport (SGLT) in the proximal convoluted tubule reabsorbs filtered glucose nearly completely under normal loads.

items.category_name: Translational motion, forces, work, energy, and equilibrium in living systems
items.stem: A 2 kg object is raised vertically at constant velocity through a height of 3 m (take g = 10 m/s^2). The work done against gravity is:

source.location: Work, energy, and energy resources (gravitational PE)

items.rationale: Work against gravity equals mgh = 2 x 10 x 3 = 60 J; at constant velocity net work is zero, but work against gravity is 60 J.

items.category_name: Importance of fluids for the circulation of blood, gas movement, and gas exchange
items.stem: An incompressible fluid flows steadily from a wide pipe into a narrower section. By the continuity equation, the fluid's speed in the narrow section:

source.location: Fluid dynamics: the equation of continuity

items.rationale: Continuity (A1v1 = A2v2) requires that, for constant flow rate, a smaller cross-sectional area produces a higher velocity.

items.category_name: Electrochemistry and electrical circuits and their elements
items.stem: Two identical resistors, each of resistance R, connected in series have an equivalent resistance of:

source.location: Circuits: resistors in series and parallel

items.rationale: Series resistances add directly: R_eq = R + R = 2R.

items.category_name: How light and sound interact with matter
items.stem: A sound source moves toward a stationary observer. Relative to the emitted frequency, the observer perceives a frequency that is:

source.location: Sound: the Doppler effect

items.rationale: For an approaching source, wavefronts are compressed, raising the observed frequency (Doppler shift).

items.category_name: Atoms, nuclear decay, electronic structure, and atomic chemical behavior
items.stem: In beta-minus (beta-) decay, a neutron converts to a proton. How do the atomic number (Z) and mass number (A) of the nucleus change?

options.text: Z increases by 1; A is unchanged
Z decreases by 2; A decreases by 4
Z is unchanged; A decreases by 1
Z decreases by 1; A is unchanged

source.location: Nuclear chemistry: types of radioactive decay

items.rationale: Beta-minus decay (n -> p + e-) raises the proton count by one while total nucleon number stays the same.

items.category_name: Unique nature of water and its solutions
items.stem: An aqueous solution has a hydrogen-ion concentration [H+] = 1 x 10^-4 M. Its pH is:

source.location: Acid-base equilibria: pH and pOH

items.category_name: Nature of molecules and intermolecular interactions
items.stem: The unusually high boiling point of water compared with similar-mass molecules is primarily due to:

options.text: The strength of the O-H covalent bonds within a molecule

source.location: Liquids and solids: intermolecular forces

items.rationale: Extensive intermolecular hydrogen bonding raises the energy needed to vaporize water; intramolecular covalent bonds are not broken during boiling.

items.category_name: Separation and purification methods
items.stem: In normal-phase column chromatography (polar stationary phase, relatively nonpolar mobile phase), a strongly polar compound will tend to:

options.text: Be retained longest and elute last
Elute first because it moves fastest
Not interact with the stationary phase at all

source.location: Structure determination and lab techniques: chromatography

items.rationale: On a polar stationary phase, polar analytes adsorb more strongly and travel more slowly, eluting last.

items.category_name: Structure, function, and reactivity of biologically relevant molecules
items.stem: Two molecules that are non-superimposable mirror images of each other are best classified as:

source.location: Stereochemistry: enantiomers and chirality

items.rationale: Enantiomers are stereoisomers that are non-superimposable mirror images; diastereomers are not mirror images.

items.category_name: Principles of chemical thermodynamics and kinetics
items.stem: Adding a catalyst to a reaction at constant temperature will:

options.text: Lower the activation energy and speed both the forward and reverse reactions
Shift the equilibrium toward the products
Make the reaction's dG more negative
Increase the value of the equilibrium constant

source.location: Kinetics: catalysis and activation energy

items.rationale: A catalyst lowers Ea and accelerates forward and reverse rates equally; it does not change dG, K, or the equilibrium position.

items.category_name: Sensing the environment
items.stem: In the human eye, transduction of light into neural signals occurs in the:

options.text: Photoreceptors (rods and cones) of the retina

source.location: Sensation and perception: vision

items.rationale: Rods and cones convert light into neural signals (transduction); the cornea and lens focus light but do not transduce it.

items.category_name: Making sense of the environment
items.stem: A person holds a 7-digit number in mind just long enough to dial it, then forgets it. This best illustrates:

source.location: Memory: stages of memory (short-term/working memory)

items.rationale: Brief, limited-capacity retention for immediate use is the defining feature of short-term/working memory.

items.category_name: Responding to the world
items.stem: The James-Lange theory of emotion proposes that the subjective experience of an emotion:

options.text: Arises from perceiving one's own physiological (bodily) response to a stimulus
Occurs simultaneously with, but independently of, physiological arousal
Requires arousal plus a cognitive label for that arousal
Occurs entirely before any bodily change

source.location: Emotion: theories of emotion (James-Lange, Cannon-Bard, Schachter-Singer)

items.rationale: James-Lange holds that bodily responses come first and the emotion is our perception of them; option B is Cannon-Bard and option C is Schachter-Singer.

items.category_name: Individual influences on behavior
items.stem: In Pavlov's classical conditioning, a previously neutral stimulus that elicits a response only after being paired with food is the:

source.location: Learning: classical conditioning

items.rationale: After repeated pairing with the unconditioned stimulus (food), the neutral stimulus becomes the conditioned stimulus that triggers a conditioned response.

items.category_name: Social processes that influence human behavior
items.stem: Individuals exerting less effort on a task when working in a group than when working alone illustrates:

source.location: Social psychology: group behavior (social loafing)

items.rationale: Social loafing is the reduction of individual effort when responsibility is diffused across a group.

items.category_name: Attitude and behavior change
items.stem: According to the elaboration likelihood model, persuasion through the 'central route' depends primarily on:

options.text: Careful evaluation of the quality of the arguments
Superficial cues such as the speaker's attractiveness

source.location: Attitudes and persuasion: elaboration likelihood model

items.rationale: The central route involves effortful, thoughtful processing of argument content; peripheral cues define the peripheral route.

items.category_name: Self-identity
items.stem: A student's belief in their own capability to succeed at a specific upcoming task is best described as:

source.location: Personality / motivation: Bandura's self-efficacy

items.rationale: Self-efficacy is task-specific confidence in one's ability to succeed; self-esteem is a broader self-evaluation.

items.category_name: Social thinking
items.stem: Attributing another driver's error to their bad character while attributing your own identical error to traffic conditions best illustrates:

options.text: The fundamental attribution error (with actor-observer asymmetry)

source.location: Social cognition: attribution and attributional biases

items.rationale: Overweighting dispositional causes for others' behavior while favoring situational causes for one's own reflects the fundamental attribution error / actor-observer bias.

items.category_name: Social interactions
items.stem: Adjusting one's behavior to control the impression others form, as in Goffman's dramaturgical view, is termed:

source.location: Socialization and interaction: dramaturgy and self-presentation

items.rationale: Impression management / self-presentation is the dramaturgical idea that people manage how others perceive them.

items.category_name: Understanding social structure
items.stem: A sociologist who studies how social institutions interlock to maintain societal stability is working within which theoretical perspective?

source.location: An introduction to sociology: theoretical perspectives

items.rationale: Structural functionalism analyzes how institutions contribute to social stability and equilibrium; conflict theory emphasizes power and inequality.

items.category_name: Demographic characteristics and processes
items.stem: The demographic transition model predicts that as a society industrializes:

options.text: Death rates fall first, followed later by falling birth rates
Birth and death rates both rise indefinitely
Birth rates fall well before death rates
Both rates remain constant over time

source.location: Population, urbanization, and the environment: demographic transition

items.rationale: In the classic model, improvements first reduce mortality; fertility declines later, producing a period of rapid population growth in between.

items.category_name: Social inequality
items.stem: Systematically worse health outcomes among lower-socioeconomic-status groups, driven by unequal access to care and resources, are best described as:

options.text: Health disparities arising from social determinants of health

source.location: Health and medicine: social epidemiology and health disparities

items.rationale: Socioeconomically patterned differences in health outcomes are health disparities produced by social determinants, not by chance or biology alone.
